title: Economic growth
summary: Economic growth is one of the central macroeconomic objectives. This lesson pins down exactly what growth means — separating actual growth (using spare capacity) from potential growth (expanding capacity) — shows how to illustrate both on a production possibilities curve AND on an AD/AS diagram, works through the causes of growth (more and better factors of production, investment, technology and productivity), and evaluates its consequences for living standards, inequality, the environment and sustainability.
learningObjectives: Define economic growth and distinguish clearly between actual growth (short-run) and potential growth (long-run).
Illustrate both actual and potential growth on a production possibilities curve (PPC) and on an AD/AS diagram.
Explain the causes of growth: increases in the quantity and quality of factors of production, investment, technology and productivity.
Evaluate the consequences of economic growth, weighing higher living standards against inequality, environmental costs and sustainability, and distinguish growth from development.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Growing the Economic Pie
simpleExplanation.summary: Economic growth means a country's real output of goods and services rises over time. It can happen in two ways: by putting idle resources back to work (actual growth) or by increasing the economy's maximum productive capacity (potential growth).
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a pizza restaurant. On a quiet night it can serve more pizzas simply by filling its empty tables and firing up ovens that were sitting idle — that is actual growth, using up spare capacity. But once every table is full and every oven is running, the only way to serve more is to buy bigger ovens or open a second branch — that raises the maximum it can ever produce, which is potential growth.
steps: Economic growth is the percentage increase in real GDP over a period. 'Real' strips out inflation so we measure the true change in the volume of output.
Actual growth uses up spare capacity: a move from inside the PPC towards it, or a rightward shift of AD/SRAS towards full employment.
Potential growth expands capacity: an outward shift of the whole PPC, or a rightward shift of LRAS.

Weigh the consequences: growth can raise living standards and cut unemployment, but may widen inequality, damage the environment and threaten sustainability — and growth is not the same as development.

flashcards.back: An increase in the real output of an economy over time, measured as the percentage change in real Gross Domestic Product (GDP).
The total value of all final goods and services produced in an economy over a period, adjusted for inflation. It reflects the actual volume of output, not just its money value.
An increase in real GDP achieved by using previously idle resources or existing resources more efficiently. Shown by a movement from inside the PPC towards the curve, or a rightward shift of AD/SRAS.
An increase in the productive capacity of the economy, caused by more or better factors of production. Shown by an outward shift of the PPC, or a rightward shift of the LRAS curve.

A movement from INSIDE the PPC to a point ON it shows actual growth; an outward SHIFT of the whole curve shows potential growth. Both are different from a simple movement along the curve, which shows opportunity cost.
The long-run aggregate supply curve represents the economy's potential (full-employment) output. A rightward shift of LRAS is potential economic growth.
Output per unit of input, e.g. output per worker per hour. Rising productivity lets the same resources produce more and is a key driver of potential growth.
The stock of skills, knowledge and health embodied in the workforce. Education, training and healthcare raise human capital, productivity and potential growth.
Physical capital is the stock of man-made aids to production (machinery, factories, infrastructure). Investment (capital formation) adds to this stock and is a major cause of growth.
A cause (source) makes growth happen, e.g. technological progress or investment. A consequence is an effect of growth, e.g. higher living standards or pollution. Never mix the two up in an answer.

Growth is a rise in real output (a quantitative measure). Development is broader and qualitative — improvements in living standards, health, education and freedoms. Growth can occur without development.
Growth that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. It weighs the environmental and resource costs of expanding output.

quickCheck.prompt: An economy moves from a point INSIDE its PPC to a point ON the curve. This best illustrates:
options: Potential growth
Actual growth
A fall in the price level
A movement of the whole curve

quickCheck.prompt: Which single event is best shown as an outward SHIFT of the entire PPC rather than a movement towards it?
options: A fall in cyclical unemployment
A rise in consumer confidence raising spending
A major improvement in production technology
Using idle factories during a recovery
quickCheck.answer: A major improvement in production technology

quickCheck.prompt: On an AD/AS diagram, long-run (potential) economic growth is shown by:
options: A rightward shift of AD only
A rightward shift of SRAS only
A rightward shift of the LRAS curve
A movement up the SRAS curve
quickCheck.answer: A rightward shift of the LRAS curve

quickCheck.prompt: A country's real GDP rises by 5%, but the extra income goes almost entirely to the richest households while pollution worsens. The most accurate statement is:
options: Economic growth has occurred, but it may not have raised development
No economic growth has occurred because inequality rose
This is potential growth, not actual growth
Growth and development always move together
quickCheck.answer: Economic growth has occurred, but it may not have raised development

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is a CONSEQUENCE of economic growth rather than a CAUSE of it?
options: Investment in new machinery
An improvement in the workforce's skills
Higher average material living standards
Advances in technology

faq.q: What is the difference between actual and potential growth?
faq.a: Actual growth is an increase in real output that the economy achieves right now — usually by bringing idle or under-used resources back into use. It is a movement from inside the PPC towards the frontier, or a rightward shift of AD/SRAS towards full employment. Potential growth is an increase in the maximum the economy could ever produce — its productive capacity. It comes from more or better factors of production and is shown by the whole PPC shifting outwards, or the LRAS curve shifting right. An economy can have actual growth without potential growth (a recovery) and potential growth without much actual growth (new capacity that is not yet fully used).

faq.q: Is economic growth always a good thing?
faq.a: Not automatically. Growth is usually linked to higher incomes and lower unemployment, but it can carry real costs: environmental degradation and pollution, depletion of non-renewable resources, and wider income inequality if the gains are captured by a narrow group. If growth is demand-driven and the economy is near capacity, it can also cause demand-pull inflation. This is why economists increasingly stress sustainable and inclusive growth, which weighs these environmental and social effects rather than treating a higher GDP figure as success in itself.

faq.q: Does economic growth mean the same as economic development?
faq.a: No. Growth is purely quantitative — a rise in real GDP. Development is broader and qualitative: improvements in living standards, health, education, gender equality and personal freedoms. Growth can support development by generating income and tax revenue, but the two do not always move together. A country can grow while poverty, inequality or environmental damage worsen, so a higher GDP does not guarantee that people's lives have improved.

sections.content: Economic growth is a central objective for almost every government, and it is one of the most tested ideas in Paper 1. But 'growth' hides two quite different things, and examiners reward students who keep them apart. In this lesson we define growth precisely, show how to draw it on BOTH the production possibilities curve and the AD/AS model, trace where growth comes from, and weigh what it does — for good and ill — to living standards, inequality and the environment.
What economic growth means: actual vs potential

Economic growth is the percentage increase in a country's real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) over a period, usually a quarter or a year. We use 'real' GDP — output valued at constant prices — so that we measure a genuine rise in the volume of goods and services, not just a rise in prices. Behind that single headline number lie two distinct ideas that you must never conflate.

items: **Actual growth (short-run):** an increase in real output the economy actually achieves, typically by taking up the 'slack' — using previously unemployed or under-used resources, or using existing resources more efficiently.

**Potential growth (long-run):** an increase in the economy's productive capacity — the maximum it could produce at full employment. This requires more or better factors of production, and can exist even if that new capacity is not yet fully used.

sections.question: The real GDP of Econland was $450 billion in 2022 and $468 billion in 2023. Calculate Econland's rate of economic growth in 2023, and state whether this figure alone tells you if the growth was actual or potential.
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — identify the values.** Real GDP in 2022 = $450bn; real GDP in 2023 = $468bn.

**Step 2 — apply the formula.** Growth rate = ((468 − 450) / 450) × 100.

**Step 3 — compute.** Change in real GDP = $18bn. Growth rate = (18 / 450) × 100 = 0.04 × 100 = **4%**.

**Step 4 — interpret.** The 4% figure tells us real output rose, but it does NOT by itself reveal whether this was actual growth (idle resources coming back into use, e.g. after a recession) or potential growth (a genuine expansion of capacity). To know which, we would need to see whether the economy was moving towards its PPC/full-employment output, or whether that frontier itself shifted outwards. This distinction is exactly what examiners test.

sections.content: Illustrating growth (1): the production possibilities curve

The PPC shows the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce when resources are used fully and efficiently with fixed technology. It gives us the cleanest picture of the actual-versus-potential distinction, and the two are shown by completely different movements.

items: **Actual growth on the PPC:** a movement from a point INSIDE the curve towards a point ON the curve. Idle resources (e.g. cyclically unemployed workers or factories) are brought into use, so real output rises without the frontier moving.

**Potential growth on the PPC:** an outward SHIFT of the entire curve. The maximum attainable output has risen because there are now more or better factors of production, so points that were previously beyond the frontier become attainable.

**Not the same as a movement ALONG the curve:** sliding from one point on the frontier to another shows opportunity cost (more of one good means less of the other), NOT growth. Confusing this with a shift is a classic error.

sections.content: The AD/AS model tells the same story in a second way, and a strong Paper 1 answer can show growth on both diagrams. Here the key is which curve moves.

items: **Actual growth on AD/AS:** a rightward shift of AD (e.g. rising consumer confidence or investment) or of SRAS (e.g. lower oil prices) raises real GDP towards the full-employment level. Real output rises, but the economy's potential (LRAS) has not changed.

**Potential growth on AD/AS:** a rightward shift of the LRAS curve. Full-employment output (Yp) rises, so the economy can sustain a higher real GDP without generating inflation. On a monetarist/new-classical diagram LRAS is vertical at potential output; on a Keynesian diagram it is the horizontal full-capacity output that moves outward.
**Putting them together:** an outward PPC shift and a rightward LRAS shift are two representations of the SAME event — an increase in productive capacity. Being able to link them is a top-band skill.

sections.content: Whenever a question says 'economic growth', decide in your first sentence whether you mean actual (short-run) or potential (long-run) growth, then choose the matching diagram move: inside-to-on the PPC / AD or SRAS shift for actual; outward PPC shift / LRAS shift for potential. Label everything — axes, curves, the specific point or shift — and then EXPLAIN in words what the reader is looking at. A labelled diagram that is not explained does not reach the top band.

Potential growth comes down to one idea: increasing the quantity OR improving the quality of the factors of production, so the economy can produce more. Actual growth, by contrast, is mostly about how fully the existing capacity is used. The main causes of potential growth are the following.

items: **More factors of production (quantity):** a larger labour force (e.g. through population growth or immigration), the discovery of new natural resources (land), and a growing capital stock all raise capacity.

**Better factors of production (quality):** improvements in human capital through education, training and healthcare make workers more productive; better-quality capital equipment produces more per machine.

**Investment:** spending by firms and government on new physical capital — machinery, factories and infrastructure such as roads, ports and broadband — adds to the capital stock and is a central engine of growth.
**Technology:** technological progress lets the economy produce more output from the same inputs, and is one of the most important long-run drivers of growth.
**Productivity:** rising output per unit of input (often the result of investment, technology and human-capital gains) means the same resources yield more, shifting LRAS and the PPC outwards.
**Institutions and incentives:** stable government, secure property rights and a reliable legal system encourage the investment and entrepreneurship that underpin all of the above.

sections.question: Using a long-run AD/AS diagram, illustrate and explain how a government policy of large-scale investment in high-speed internet infrastructure might affect economic growth and the average price level.
sections.solution:
**Diagram.** Draw an AD/AS diagram with initial equilibrium E1 where AD1, SRAS1 and LRAS1 intersect, at price level P1 and potential output Yp1. Then shift LRAS rightwards (LRAS1 → LRAS2). Because lower production costs accompany the better infrastructure, also shift SRAS rightwards (SRAS1 → SRAS2). The new long-run equilibrium E2 is where AD1 meets LRAS2, at a HIGHER potential output (Yp2) and a LOWER price level (P2).

**Explanation.**
1. **Starting point:** the economy begins at its long-run equilibrium (Yp1, P1).
2. **Nature of the policy:** high-speed internet is national infrastructure — a form of physical capital. This is investment that raises the economy's productive capacity, i.e. a CAUSE of potential growth.
3. **Effect on the curves:** greater capacity shifts LRAS right (LRAS1 → LRAS2). As firms enjoy faster communication and cheaper logistics, their production costs fall, shifting SRAS right (SRAS1 → SRAS2).
4. **New equilibrium:** potential output rises (Yp2 > Yp1) while the price level falls (P2 < P1). This is non-inflationary potential growth.

**Conclusion.** The policy generates long-run (potential) economic growth and, by lowering costs, tends to reduce the average price level rather than raise it — a contrast with demand-driven actual growth, which can be inflationary near full capacity.

sections.content: Growth is a leading policy goal because of its benefits, but a balanced, evaluative answer — the kind that earns higher-order marks — must weigh those benefits against real costs. The effect on economic well-being depends on HOW growth is achieved and WHO gains from it.

items: **Benefits — living standards:** higher average real incomes and improved material living standards; lower unemployment as firms hire to meet rising output; higher government tax revenue to fund healthcare, education and merit goods; a route out of poverty for many households.

**Costs — inequality:** if the gains from growth accrue mainly to owners of capital or high-skilled workers, income and wealth inequality can widen, so average GDP per head rises while many are no better off.

**Costs — environment and sustainability:** more production and consumption can bring pollution, congestion, and depletion of non-renewable resources; growth that ignores these threatens SUSTAINABILITY — the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

**Costs — inflation and other social costs:** demand-driven growth near full capacity can cause demand-pull inflation; rapid change can also bring stress, longer working hours and structural unemployment in declining industries.

**Growth is not development:** a rising GDP does not guarantee better lives. Development also depends on health, education, equality and the environment — so growth is necessary but not sufficient for improved well-being.

**Confusing actual with potential growth** — actual growth uses up spare capacity (inside-to-on the PPC; AD/SRAS shift); potential growth expands capacity (outward PPC shift; LRAS shift). State which you mean.
**Showing growth as a movement ALONG the PPC instead of a SHIFT of it** — sliding along the frontier shows opportunity cost, not growth. Potential growth is the whole curve moving outward.

**Treating an inside-the-PPC point as if it were on the curve** — from inside, output can rise with little or no opportunity cost because idle resources are being used; that is actual, not potential, growth.

**Confusing causes with consequences** — investment, technology and better factors of production are CAUSES; higher living standards, inequality and pollution are CONSEQUENCES. Do not list a cause as an effect.
**Equating growth with development** — a higher real GDP is not automatically higher well-being; ignoring inequality, the environment and sustainability loses evaluation marks.
**Drawing a diagram but not explaining it** — an unlabelled or unexplained PPC/AD-AS diagram caps you in a lower band even if it is technically correct.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain, using a production possibilities curve AND an AD/AS diagram, the difference between actual and potential economic growth. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
Economic growth is an increase in real GDP over time, but it takes two forms that must be distinguished. Actual growth is a rise in real output the economy currently achieves by using idle or under-used resources; potential growth is an increase in the economy's productive capacity — the maximum it could produce at full employment — driven by more or better factors of production.

*[Diagram 1 — PPC: axes 'Capital goods' (vertical) and 'Consumer goods' (horizontal); a curve bowed outwards, PPC1. Mark point U INSIDE PPC1 and point A ON PPC1. Show an arrow from U to A to represent actual growth. Then draw a second curve PPC2 outside PPC1 and mark point B on PPC2, with an arrow from A to B to represent potential growth.]*

On the PPC, actual growth is the movement from U (inside the curve, where resources are unemployed) to A (on the curve): real output rises because idle resources are put to work, but the frontier itself does not move. Potential growth is the outward SHIFT of the whole curve, PPC1 → PPC2, so combinations once beyond reach (point B) become attainable — the maximum output has risen. A mere movement ALONG a single curve would show opportunity cost, not growth.

*[Diagram 2 — AD/AS: axes 'Average price level' (vertical) and 'Real GDP' (horizontal); AD1, SRAS1 and a vertical LRAS1 at potential output Yp1, meeting at price level P1. For actual growth, shift AD1 → AD2 to raise real GDP towards Yp1. For potential growth, shift LRAS1 → LRAS2 to a higher potential output Yp2.]*

On the AD/AS diagram, actual growth is shown by a rightward shift of AD (AD1 → AD2), raising real GDP towards the full-employment level Yp1 without moving potential output. Potential growth is shown by a rightward shift of the LRAS curve (LRAS1 → LRAS2), raising full-employment output from Yp1 to Yp2, so the economy can sustain a higher real GDP. The outward PPC shift and the LRAS shift are two representations of the same underlying event: an increase in productive capacity from more or better factors of production, investment, technology or productivity.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
- **9–10:** the demands of the question are addressed, relevant economic theory is FULLY explained, relevant terms are used appropriately THROUGHOUT, and diagram(s) are included and FULLY explained.
- **7–8:** the demands of the question are explained, relevant terms are mostly used appropriately, and diagram(s) are included and explained.
- **5–6:** the demands of the question are partially addressed, there is a PARTLY explained response, some relevant terms are used, and diagram(s) are included.
- **3–4:** there is a DESCRIPTIVE response, and some relevant terms are used.
- **1–2:** the response is not relevant.

**Why this answer reaches the top band (9–10):**
- ✅ Addresses the demand directly: it explains the DIFFERENCE between actual and potential growth, not just each in isolation.
- ✅ Theory fully explained: both diagrams are used to DERIVE the distinction (inside-to-on vs outward shift; AD shift vs LRAS shift), not merely mentioned.
- ✅ Relevant terms used throughout: actual growth, potential growth, productive capacity, full employment, LRAS, factors of production.
- ✅ BOTH diagrams included AND fully explained: axes, curves and points (U, A, B; AD1/AD2, LRAS1/LRAS2, Yp1/Yp2) are labelled and then referenced in the written explanation.
An otherwise similar response that DESCRIBED the two types and drew the diagrams without explaining what the movements mean would sit in the 3–4 ('descriptive') or 5–6 ('partly explained') band. The single thing that lifts this into 9–10 is EXPLAINING each diagram rather than just drawing it.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands here: show growth on BOTH diagrams AND explain every movement, instead of leaving a labelled diagram to speak for itself.

The 2022 course is built around key concepts, and this lesson draws on three: **change** (growth is the economy's real output changing over time), **economic well-being** (growth can raise material living standards, but only if its gains are shared and its costs contained), and **sustainability** (growth that depletes resources or damages the environment may not be maintainable for future generations). Linking a key concept to real-world material is specifically rewarded in the internal assessment, so keep these in view. This content is common to SL and HL at this level.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: use a PPC and an AD/AS diagram to explain actual vs potential growth
